schools.summary: The 'weak' is the believer (chiefly the Jewish convert) whose faith cannot yet grasp his full Christian liberty — scrupulous about meats and days, yet truly a believer — whom the strong are to receive (welcome to full fellowship), 'not to doubtful disputations,' i.e. not for wrangling over his opinions or unsettling his conscience (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Calvin): receive the weak brother in gentleness, not to judge or contend over his scruples — bearing with his infirmity in love.

themes: Him that is weak in the faith receive ye
but not to doubtful disputations
the reception of the weak believer
forbearance without wrangling

schools.summary: The strong believer is persuaded that under the gospel all foods are lawful (the ceremonial distinctions abolished), while the weak, doubtful about meats (fearing flesh might be unclean or idol-tainted), confines himself to vegetables to be safe, eating only herbs (Barnes, Robertson).
Theodoret (with Calvin): the weak (Jewish-minded) abstained from certain meats from conscience — the example concrete and practical, the matter being food.

themes: one believeth that he may eat all things
another, who is weak, eateth herbs
the difference between strong and weak
scruples over meats

schools.summary: The strong (who eats all) must not despise (hold in contempt) the weak as superstitious, and the weak (who abstains) must not judge (condemn) the strong as lawless — each tempted to a characteristic fault — for God has received both, the strong and the weak alike accepted by him (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): God has received both; neither should reject whom God has welcomed — the divine acceptance the ground of mutual forbearance.

themes: Let not him that eateth despise him that eateth not
let not him which eateth not judge him that eateth
mutual forbearance, neither despise nor judge
God hath received him

schools.summary: The believer is God's servant, not ours; to judge a fellow servant usurps the master's place — the servant answers to his own master (God), standing or falling by God's verdict, not ours; and he shall be upheld, for God is able to make him stand (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): the gracious assurance — the weak servant shall be held up, for God is able and willing to make him stand; God's power preserving his own.

themes: Who art thou that judgest another man's servant
to his own master he standeth or falleth
God is able to make him stand
the rebuke of judging another's servant

schools.summary: The verse concerns the Jewish festival days (passover, pentecost, new moons, ceremonial sabbaths), the weak still regarding them as sacred; and each must be fully persuaded in his own mind. Barnes argues strongly that 'every day alike' does NOT abolish the Christian Sabbath — the discussion touching only peculiar Jewish ceremonial days, the Lord's Day being observed by all Christians (1 Corinthians 16:2; Acts 20:7); Ellicott takes days as a matter of religious expediency.

Clarke: the word 'alike' is no part of the Greek and should not be added — the Sabbath is of lasting obligation, instituted from creation and a type of the eternal rest, so the verse cannot be made to license Sabbath-breaking; only the Jewish festivals are in view.

Calvin: in these indifferent matters each must act from full conviction, dependent on the will of God, never moving while the conscience is doubtful or vacillating; the scruple over days arising from Judaism and betraying weakness, yet to be borne with.

debates.question: Does 'esteeming every day alike' abolish the Christian Sabbath / Lord's Day?

sides.position: No — only the Jewish ceremonial festival days are in view; the Lord's Day stands, observed by all Christians
The observance of special days has no absolute sanction but is a matter of religious expediency

themes: One man esteemeth one day above another
Let every man be fully persuaded in his own mind
the difference over days
the question of the Sabbath

schools.summary: The weak who observes the day does so out of regard for the Lord (a conscientious desire to honour God), and the strong who observes it not is likewise acting to the Lord; both the eater and the abstainer give God thanks, acting from a sincere desire to please him, sanctifying their conduct by thanksgiving (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): since each, in his differing practice, sincerely seeks to honour God and gives thanks, neither should be condemned — the heart's direction toward the Lord hallows the act.

themes: He that regardeth the day, regardeth it unto the Lord
he that eateth, eateth to the Lord, for he giveth God thanks
both parties act unto the Lord
thanksgiving sanctifying the act

schools.summary: No Christian lives for himself alone, as his own master or for his own ends — his life belongs to and is governed by the Lord; and even in death the believer is not his own, his death too under the Lord's disposal and to his glory (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): since we belong wholly to the Lord in life and death, we are to act not for self but for him — and this includes bearing with our brethren rather than pleasing ourselves.

themes: none of us liveth to himself
no man dieth to himself
we live and die not to ourselves
the believer's belonging to the Lord

schools.summary: The believer's life is wholly devoted to the Lord (lived for his service and glory) and his death too is unto the Lord (an act of submission and devotion, gain in Christ); so that, living or dying, 'we are the Lord's' (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): in life and death the believer wholly belongs to Christ, Lord of both states — the great ground of comfort and of self-surrender.

themes: whether we live, we live unto the Lord
whether we live therefore, or die, we are the Lord's
living or dying, we are the Lord's
total belonging to Christ

schools.summary: Christ's death and resurrection had this design — to establish his lordship over both the dead and the living; he died and rose that he might rule over all, the departed and the living alike under his dominion (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): since Christ purchased this lordship by his death and resurrection, the believer belongs to him in life and death, and must live and die to him.

themes: to this end Christ both died, and rose, and revived
that he might be Lord both of the dead and living
Christ Lord of the dead and the living
dominion purchased by death and resurrection

schools.summary: Paul rebukes both faults — the weak who judges (condemns) and the strong who sets at nought (despises) a brother — by the decisive reason that all, both judge and judged, must stand before the judgment seat of Christ; since Christ judges all, we usurp his office in judging our brother (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the ascription of the final judgment to Christ (the judgment seat) marks his divine dignity; the coming tribunal the great check on our presumptuous judging of fellow servants.

themes: why dost thou judge thy brother? or why dost thou set at nought thy brother
we shall all stand before the judgment seat of Christ
the rebuke of judging
Christ's judgment seat

schools.summary: Paul quotes Isaiah 45:23 — God's solemn oath ('as I live') that every knee shall bow and every tongue confess to him, the universal submission of all to God; applied in the context of Christ's judgment seat, it confirms that all will bow before Christ as judge — a testimony to his deity (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): since every knee shall bow and every tongue give account to God, the judgment belongs to him alone, and our judging of brethren is presumption.

themes: As I live, saith the Lord, every knee shall bow to me
every tongue shall confess to God
the universal homage foretold
the deity of the Judge

schools.summary: The inference from vv.10-11 — since all must stand before God's tribunal, every one shall give account of himself to God, a personal individual reckoning; each answering for his own conduct, not his brother's, so we should attend to ourselves (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): since each must answer for himself, let each look to his own walk and leave his brother to God — the coming reckoning a motive to self-examination, not censoriousness.

themes: every one of us shall give account of himself to God
each shall give account to God
personal responsibility
self-examination, not judging

schools.summary: Paul concludes the first part — let us cease judging one another over these matters — and turns by a wordplay: instead of judging the brother, 'judge' (resolve) this, not to put a stumbling-block or occasion of falling in his way, the strong now specially addressed (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Theodoret): the strong must take care that their liberty does not cause the weak to stumble (act against conscience) or fall into sin — the duty of self-restraint for the brother's sake.

themes: Let us not therefore judge one another any more
no man put a stumblingblock or an occasion to fall in his brother's way
the turn from judging to not offending
the responsibility of the strong

schools.summary: Paul, siding with the strong, declares (persuaded 'in the Lord Jesus,' by the gospel's teaching) that no food is unclean in itself — the ceremonial distinctions abolished, all pure in their own nature; yet to the man who believes it unclean it is unclean, for he would act against conscience, uncleanness being subjective in the mind of the regarder (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Chrysostom (with Calvin): the weak must not be forced to violate his conscience, for to use what he thinks unclean defiles him; conscience, even when erring, binds until better instructed — and Paul forbears to press correction lest he grieve the brother.

themes: there is nothing unclean of itself
to him that esteemeth any thing to be unclean, to him it is unclean
the abolition of ceremonial uncleanness
the binding force of conscience

schools.summary: To grieve (pain, wound) a brother over food violates the law of love; and 'destroy not him for whom Christ died' warns of the ruinous tendency of such conduct — but Barnes takes 'destroy' as the danger, not the actual event, the words referring to true Christians who will nonetheless be kept by God, so the verse does not prove Christ died for any who finally perish.

Calvin: the inference that those for whom Christ died may finally perish is negatived by many Scriptures; Paul exhibits the tendency of the deed ('as far as you can do so') without affirming it effects the ruin — God will not permit the destruction of his own (John 10:27-29).

Clarke, Wesley: the words mean what they say — a man for whom Christ died may be destroyed, so stumbled as to fall and perish; this proves both that Christ died for such (general atonement) and that a believer may finally fall away; do not value thy meat more than Christ valued his life.

debates.question: Does 'destroy not him for whom Christ died' prove a true believer may finally perish?

sides.position: It warns of the tendency/danger only; the elect (true Christians) are kept by God and will not actually perish
It means what it says — one for whom Christ died may be destroyed, proving general atonement and the possibility of final falling

themes: if thy brother be grieved with thy meat, now walkest thou not charitably
Destroy not him with thy meat, for whom Christ died
the law of love
the perseverance and atonement crux

schools.summary: 'Your good' is the blessing of Christian liberty (freedom from the ceremonial law) which the strong rightly enjoy; they must not so use it as to give occasion for reproach or blasphemy, lest a good thing be evil spoken of because of the strife its misuse causes (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).
Theodoret: the strong should not let their good (liberty, or the gospel) become a cause of injury and reproach — better to forego its use than to bring it into disrepute.

themes: Let not then your good be evil spoken of
guarding Christian liberty from reproach
the misuse of liberty
the gospel's good name

schools.summary: The kingdom of God (the reign of God in the heart, true religion) does not consist in food and drink but in righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit; Barnes, Ellicott (and Calvin here) take these in the practical sense suited to the context — right dealing toward others, concord among brethren, and mutual gladness wrought by the Spirit, not primarily imputed righteousness.

Clarke (with Gill): the deeper graces — righteousness (pardon of sin and holiness of heart), peace (in the soul, from a sense of God's mercy), and joy in the Holy Ghost (solid spiritual happiness) — a genuine counterpart of heaven.

themes: the kingdom of God is not meat and drink
righteousness, and peace, and joy in the Holy Ghost
the great definition of the kingdom of God
the spiritual nature of true religion

schools.summary: He who serves Christ in righteousness, peace, and joy (making these, not food disputes, the substance of his religion) truly serves his Master, and is acceptable to God (who values these graces) and approved of men, his conduct commending itself even to the world (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Theodoret): such a one is approved by honest human judgment (not the malicious censure of the ungodly) — wherever righteousness, peace, and joy are, there the kingdom of God fully prevails.

themes: he that in these things serveth Christ
is acceptable to God, and approved of men
serving Christ pleases God and men
the commendation of a spiritual religion

schools.summary: Paul exhorts the believers to pursue (earnestly seek) the things that make for peace — concord among brethren rather than strife over indifferent matters — and the things that edify (build up) one another in faith and holiness, conduct being measured by a brother's spiritual good (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin (with Theodoret): peace and mutual edification are the standards by which the use of liberty must be judged; whatever destroys peace or hinders a brother's edifying is to be avoided.

themes: follow after the things which make for peace
things wherewith one may edify another
the pursuit of peace and edification
the rule of Christian conduct

schools.summary: 'The work of God' is the brother (the soul God is renewing) or the work of grace in him, which the strong must not pull down for the sake of food; and though 'all things are pure' (the strong man's principle conceded), it is evil for the man who eats with offence, causing a brother to stumble (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): the food is pure, but the sin lies in the uncharitable use — to eat so as to give offence is evil, the brother's edification outweighing the indulgence of liberty.

themes: For meat destroy not the work of God
All things indeed are pure; but it is evil for that man who eateth with offence
the warning not to destroy God's work
the sin in the uncharitable use

schools.summary: Paul lays down the positive rule — it is good (right, noble) to abstain from flesh, wine, or anything else if its use would cause a brother to stumble (act against conscience), be offended (scandalized), or be made weak (have his faith injured) — self-denial for love's sake (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Theodoret): the principle extends beyond food to anything indifferent that would harm a brother — love willingly forgoes its rights for the brother's good.

themes: It is good neither to eat flesh, nor to drink wine
nor any thing whereby thy brother stumbleth, or is offended, or is made weak
the rule of loving abstinence
self-denial for the brother's sake

schools.summary: 'Faith' here is the strong man's persuasion that all things are lawful, to be held privately before God, not paraded ostentatiously where it would offend; and 'happy is he that condemneth not himself in that which he alloweth' — the blessedness of a clear conscience, the man who does not condemn himself in what he approves and does (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Wesley): Christian liberty rightly understood has regard to God, and the believer should be satisfied with peace of conscience before him; happy the man who, having honestly examined his deeds, is not bitten by an accusing conscience — this assurance alone renders our works pleasing to God.

themes: Hast thou faith? have it to thyself before God
Happy is he that condemneth not himself in that thing which he alloweth
faith held before God
the blessing of a clear conscience

schools.summary: The doubter is 'condemned' if he eats (adjudged guilty, not necessarily hell) because he acts against his conscience, eating 'not of faith'; and 'whatsoever is not of faith is sin' is a general principle covering this particular case — whatever is done without the conviction that it is right is sin. Barnes and Ellicott hold the verse does NOT (against Augustine) prove every act of an unbeliever is sin; the scope is the doubting conscience.

Clarke: notes the broader Augustinian reading (taken up in Article XIII) that all works done without saving faith have the nature of sin, since without faith it is impossible to please God — while granting the immediate sense is acting against a doubting conscience.

debates.question: Does 'whatsoever is not of faith is sin' apply only to the doubting conscience or to all works done without saving faith?

sides.position: A general principle limited to this case — acting without conviction it is right; it does not prove every unbeliever's act is sin (against Augustine)
It also bears the broader Augustinian sense — all works without saving faith have the nature of sin (Article XIII)

themes: he that doubteth is damned if he eat, because he eateth not of faith
whatsoever is not of faith is sin
acting against a doubting conscience
the great maxim of faith and sin
